title: Poly(propylene)/organoclay nanocomposite formation: Influence of compatibilizer functionality and organoclay modification
abstract:
8
Poly(propylene)/organoclay nanocomposite formation:
Influence of compatibilizer functionality and organoclay
modification
Dedicated to Prof. Dr. G. Wulff on occasion of his 65th birthday
Peter Reichert, Hansjo¨rg Nitz, Stefan Klinke, Rainer Brandsch, Ralf Thomann, Rolf Mu¨lhaupt*
Freiburger Materialforschungszentrum und Institut fu¨r Makromolekulare Chemie, Stefan-Meier-Str. 31, D-79104 Freiburg
i.Br., Germany
mulhaupt@fmf.uni-freiburg.de
(Received: October 26, 1999; revised: December 1, 1999)
Introduction
Remarkable breakthroughs in catalyst and process devel-
opment as well as poly(propylene)’s attractive combina-
tion of low cost, low weight, heat distortion temperature
above 1008C, and extraordinary versatility in terms of
properties, applications, and recycling have stimulated
exceptional growth of poly(propylene) production with
respect to other thermoplastics1). Today poly(propylene)
is competing very successfully with other environmen-
tally less friendly and less versatile plastics2). In order to
improve poly(propylene)’s competiveness in engineering
resin applications, it is an important objective in poly-
Full Paper: Poly(propylene) compounds containing orga-
nophilic layered nanosilicates were prepared by means of
melt extrusion at 2108C in order to investigate the role of
silicate modification and compatibilizer addition on mor-
phology development and mechanical properties. Syn-
thetic sodium fluoromica was used as water swellable
layered silicate, which was rendered organophilic by
means of cation exchange with various protonated alkyl
amines such as butyl (C4), hexyl (C6), octyl (C8), dodecyl
(C12), hexadecyl (C16), and octadecyl (C18) amine. Inter-
layer spacing of the organophilic silicates increased with
increasing alkyl chain length of the amine. Only C12,
C16, and C18 amine modifiers in conjunction with
maleic- nhydride-grafted poly(propylene) (PP-g-MA) as
compatibilizer promoted exfoliation and self-assembly of
individual silicate
layers within the poly(propylene)
matrix. Interlayer distance increased with increasing con-
tent and increasing anhydride functionality of PP-g-MA.
Only 10 wt.-% of fluoromica modified with C16 amine in
conjunction with 20 wt.-% of PP-g-MA containing 4.2
wt.-% maleic anhydride grafts was sufficient to achieve
effective
poly(propylene)
matrix
reinforcement,
as
reflected by increase of Young’s modulus from 1490 to
3460 MPa and increase of yield stress from 33 to 44 MPa
with respect to bulk poly(propylene). Morphology, meas-
ured by means of transmission electron microscopy
(TEM) and atomic force microscopy, and mechanical
properties were examined as a function of the alkyl chain
length of the amine modifiers, PP-g-MA compatibilizer
addition and anhydride functionality of PP-g-MA.

Macromol. Mater. Eng. 275, 8–17 (2000)
 Poly(propylene)/organoclay nanocomposite formation: Influence of compatibilizer ...
9
(propylene) compounding to simultaneously increase
dimensional stability, heat distortion temperature, stiff-
ness, strength, and impact resistance without sacrificing
easy processability. The production of poly(propylene)
composites containing fiber reinforcement requires spe-
cial processing technology involving fiber impregnation
and prepreg formation3). Therefore special emphasis is
being placed upon the development of filled poly(propyl-
ene), which is produced by means of conventional melt
processing technology. Traditional fillers for poly(propyl-
ene) are calcium carbonate, talcum, glass fibers, wollasto-
nite, mica, glass beads, and wood flour. It is well known
that filler anisotropy, i.e., large length/diameter ratio
(“aspect ratio”), is especially favorable in matrix reinfor-
cement4). Although anisotropic nanofillers were found to
afford attractive combinations of stiffness and toughness,
limited commercial availability and dispersion problems
due to strong interparticle interactions of nanofillers have
limited their application5). Therefore attempts have been
made to generate anisotropic nanoparticles in-situ during
melt processing by means of in-situ exfoliation of organo-
philic layered silicates during polymerization or proces-
sing, respectively.
Polymer/organoclay
nanocomposite
formation
was
reviewed by Lagaly6), Akelah7), Giannelis8), Pinnavaia9),
and Mu¨lhaupt10). Such nanocomposites were found to
exhibit unique property combinations, e.g., higher heat
distortion temperature combined with higher stiffness,
strength, and improved barrier properties such as lower
gas and liquid permeabilities. Recently, also improved
flame retardency was attributed to nanocomposite forma-
tion11). In contrast to talcum, which is a neutral layered
magnesium silicate, anionic layered magnesium silicates,
known as hectorites, are formed when a part of the mag-
nesium is replaced by monovalent cations such as
lithium. Since sodium cations are present in the interlayer
galleries, such layered silicates are water-swellable. Ion
exchange of sodium cations for alkyl ammonium cations
renders the layers hydrophobic. Polymer nanocomposites
are formed in-situ by exposing organophilic layered sili-
cates to shear forces either during polymerization or dur-
ing processing. The chemistry of organophilic clays was
pioneered by Hofmann12) and Weiss13) more than forty
years ago. Recent interest in nanocomposites was stimu-
lated by research at Toyota, introducing polyamide nano-
composites as engineering plastics during the late 1980’s
and early 1990’s14–17). Today nanocomposite formation
represents an attractive route to upgrade and diversify
well-known polymeric materials and to afford unique
property profiles10, 18–20). For example, polyamide 6 nano-
composite films with improved barrier properties, e.g.,
reduced oxygen permeability, have been introduced
recently21).
According to Okada and coworkers22–24), poly(propyl-
ene) nanocomposites were obtained when organophilic
clay such as montmorillonite, which was rendered orga-
nophilic by means of ion exchange of intergallery sodium
cations by protonated octadecylamine (ODA), was melt-
compounded with PP in the presence of oligomers and
preferably
maleic
anhydride-grafted
poly(propylene).
Although TEM images and investigations by means of
dynamic mechanical analysis supported matrix reinforce-
ment and formation of nanocomposites, limited informa-
tion was available concerning the toughness/stiffness bal-
ance of such poly(propylene) nanocomposites. In com-
parison to conventional filled poly(propylene), poly(pro-
pylene) nanocomposites require much lower filler content
in order to achieve a Young’s modulus above 3300
MPa25). Recently, Montell North America and General
Motors Research and Development announced the devel-
opment of thermoplastic olefin elastomer-based poly(pro-
pylene) nanocomposites which are considered to compete
with PC/ABS blends and other traditional thermoplastics.
Since a low percentage of organophilic layered silicates
is sufficient to achieve reinforcement, nanocomposites
are up to 30% lighter than competitive resins. Moreover,
due to low filler content, painted parts were claimed to
exhibit better surface appearance25).
A rather large number of parameters can influence nan-
composite formation by means of melt compounding: (1)
molecular architecture of the alkyl ammonium cation
used in cation exchange, (2) additives present during sili-
cate modification, (3) processing temperature, (4) shear
rates, (5) compatibilizer type and content, and (6) poly-
mer viscosity. Therefore we have examined the influence
of the alkyl chain length of protonated alkyl-substituted
primary amines, which were used to render sodium fluor-
omica organophilic, as well as the influence of the maleic
anyhdride-grafted poly(propylene) (PP-g-MA) compati-
bilizer and processing conditions on nanocomposite mor-
phology, imaged by means of transmission electron
microscopy, and polymer properties such as stiffness,
strength, and impact resistance.
Experimental part
Materials
The synthetic clay used for our studies is a three layer sili-
cate that is produced by CO-OP CHEMICAL Co., Japan, by
heating together talcum and Na2SiF6. The trade name of this
material is SOMASIF ME100 (ME). The cation exchange
capacity (CEC) of ME is 0.7–0.8 meq/g (meg = 10–3 equiva-
lents). The interlayer spacing of the virgin ME is 0.95 nm.
The poly(propylene) (PP) used for this studies was obtained
from Borealis, the trade name of the PP is PP HC 001 A-B1,
its melt flow index (MFI) is 3.2 g/10 min, its melting point
1638C. The maleic anhydrid-modified PP oligomer (termed
PP-g-MA) was Epolene E43 (MA content 2.9 wt.-%; M
—
n =
8800; polydispersity index M
—
w/M
—
n = 2.6; Tm = 156.78C; East-
man, abbreviated as compatibilizer E) and Hostaprime HC5

in order to achieve a Young’s modulus above 3300

Motors Research and Development announced the devel-

pylene) nanocomposites which are considered to compete

is sufficient to achieve reinforcement, nanocomposites
are up to 30% lighter than competitive resins. Moreover,
due to low filler content, painted parts were claimed to
exhibit better surface appearance25).
A rather large number of parameters can influence nan-
composite formation by means of melt compounding: (1)
molecular architecture of the alkyl ammonium cation
used in cation exchange, (2) additives present during sili-
cate modification, (3) processing temperature, (4) shear
rates, (5) compatibilizer type and content, and (6) poly-
mer viscosity. Therefore we have examined the influence
of the alkyl chain length of protonated alkyl-substituted
primary amines, which were used to render sodium fluor-
omica organophilic, as well as the influence of the maleic
anyhdride-grafted poly(propylene) (PP-g-MA) compati-

The synthetic clay used for our studies is a three layer sili-
cate that is produced by CO-OP CHEMICAL Co., Japan, by
heating together talcum and Na2SiF6. The trade name of this
material is SOMASIF ME100 (ME). The cation exchange
capacity (CEC) of ME is 0.7–0.8 meq/g (meg = 10–3 equiva-
lents). The interlayer spacing of the virgin ME is 0.95 nm.
The poly(propylene) (PP) used for this studies was obtained
from Borealis, the trade name of the PP is PP HC 001 A-B1,
its melt flow index (MFI) is 3.2 g/10 min, its melting point
1638C. The maleic anhydrid-modified PP oligomer (termed
PP-g-MA) was Epolene E43 (MA content 2.9 wt.-%; M
man, abbreviated as compatibilizer E) and Hostaprime HC5
n = 4000; polydispersity index M
n = 8; Tm = 151.78C; Hoechst AG, abbreviated as compati-
Preparation of organophilic layered silicates
Typically 1.20 mol of the amine and 1.20 mol HCl were dis-
persed in 20 l of 808C hot water. Then 1 kg of the ME sili-
cate was added to this mixture and stirred for 30 min. The
precipitate was filtered in a filter press and washed with
100 l of hot water. After the silicate has been dried at 808 C
for 24 h, it was ground in a mill (Retsch ZM100). Wide-
angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) traces of the organophilic
layered silicates are displayed in Fig. 1 and the interlayer
spacings are listed in Tab. 1.
Compounding and preparation of poly(propylene)
PP powder and the organoclay were premixed in a tumbling
mixer together with 0.25 wt.-% of stabilizer (Irganox1010/
together with 20 wt.-% of PP-g-MA in a corotating twin
screw extruder (Werner & Pfleiderer; ZSK 25) at 190–
2308C and at 300 r.p.m. The obtained strands were pelletized
and dried at 808C. The dried pellets were injection-molded
into test bars for tensile and impact testing. The temperature
of the cylinder was 200–2358C and the mold was at 808C.
The degree of swelling and the interlayer distance of the ME
in the nanocomposites and composites were studied by
means of wide-angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) using a Sie-
mens D500 apparatus with the CuKa radiation (k =
1.5418 nm) and a scanning rate of 0.3 8/min.
Transmission electron microscopy
The morphology of the samples was examined by transmis-
sion electron microscopy (TEM). For TEM measurements,
ultrathin sections were prepared at –808C with an Ultracut
E, Reichert & Jung, ultramicrotome using a diamond knife.
The measurements were carried out on a LEO 912 Omega
The AFM measurements were done on a NANOSCOPE III
(Digital Instruments, Inc.) in the height mode. For this analy-
sis the samples were molten and annealed isothermally at
2008C between two cover glasses and quenched to room
temperature. Then they were etched with a mixture of 0.1 g
KMnO4, 10 g o-H3PO4 and 10 ml conc. H2SO4, washed with
water, and dried. This procedure removes the PP, so that the
ME was detectable on the sample surface.
The tensile modulus of the composites and nanocomposites
was measured with an Instron 4202 (ISO/DP 527) and the
notched impact strength was tested with an impact tester
Zwick, model 5102 (ISO 180/1A). The data were taken at
room temperature without preconditioning of the samples.
The synthetic fluorine-containing hectorite (SOMASIF
ME100), obtained by heating talcum together with
Na2SiF6 and abbreviated as ME, was rendered organophi-
lic by means of ion exchange of sodium cations, located in
the interlayer galleries, by protonated primary alkyl
amines, where alkyl was butyl (C4), hexyl (C6), octyl
(C8), dodecyl (C12), hexadecyl (C16), and octadecyl
(C18). The samples of the organophilic silicates are
denoted as MECn where n represents the number of C
atoms of the alkyl amine substitutent. The influence of the
alkyl chain length on the silicate interlayer distance was
examined by means of wide angle X-ray scattering
(WAXS). Typical WAXS traces are displayed in Fig. 1.
Interlayer distances and amine content are listed in Tab. 1.
It is apparent from Tab. 1 that the silicate interlayer dis-
tance increased with increasing alkyl chain length of the
alkyl-substituted primary amine, which was used to ren-
Interlayer spacing and amine content of the modified
WAXS traces of the organophilic layered silicates
MECn, rendered organophilic by means of ion exchange with
Poly(propylene)/organoclay nanocomposite formation: Influence of compatibilizer ...
der ME silicate organophilic. While ion exchange with
protonated C4, C6, and C8 amines gave a very similar
interlayer distance of about 1.3 nm, the amines with
higher molar mass afforded much larger interlayer spa-
cing of about 1.7 nm for C16 and 2 nm for C18, with
respect to 0.95 nm for the non-modified ME silicate.
Obviously, effective silicate modification requires long
alkyl chain length S C8. This is in accordance with ear-
lier observations of Hofmann and Weiss, who reported an
odd-even effect and much more effective interlayer
separation when using alkyl substituents larger than
Poly(propylene) composites and nanocomposites were
prepared by melt compounding of poly(propylene) in the
presence of the organophilic fluoromica rendered organo-
philic by means of ion exchange with protonated alklyl
amines. Poly(propylene) melt compounding was per-
formed at 190–2308C using a corotating twin-screw
extruder (Werner & Pfleiderer ZSK25). Poly(propylene)
with a melt flow index (MFI) of 3.2 g/10 min was com-
Mechanical properties of composites and nanocomposites compared to poly(propylene)
pounded together with the silicates listed in Tab. 1 in the
absence and presence of compatibilizer such as maleic
anydride-grafted poly(propylene) (PP-g-MA) containing
2.9 wt.-% (Epolene E43 from Eastman Co., abbreviated
as E) or 4.2 wt.-% maleic anhydride (Hostaprime HC5,
abbreviated as H). Such PP-g-MA compatibilizers are
well known to improve interfacial adhesion between
poly(propylene) and fillers. PP-g-MA compatibilizers
were used by Okada and coworkers to pepare poly(propy-
lene) nanocomposites24). In order to examine the influ-
ence of the alkyl chain length of the silicate modifier on
morphology development and mechanical properties, the
content of the organophilic layered silicates MECn was
varied from 0 to 10 wt.-% at a high PP-g-MA compatibi-
lizer content of 20 wt.-%. For comparison, compatibili-
zer-free compounds were also investigated in this work.
The influence of the anhydride content of PP-g-MA was
studied by comparing PP-g-MA containing 2.9 wt.-%
(compatibilizer E) and 4.2 wt.-% (compatibilizer H) of
grafted maleic anhydride. Compositions and properties of
the poly(propylene) compounds are listed in Tab. 2.
The dramatic impact of both PP-g-MA compatibilizer
addition and alkyl chain length on poly(propylene)’s
Young’s modulus and yield stress is illustrated in Fig. 2
and Fig. 3 for different MECn filler contents varying
between 0 and 10 wt.-%. From Fig. 2 it is apparent that
PP-g-MA compatibilizer addition afforded substantially
higher stiffness. Moreover, silicate modification with C12
amine gave much larger stiffness with respect to that
obtained using C8 ammonium-modified silicate MEC8.
While the modulus increase was very similar for com-
pounds prepared in the presence of both E and H type
compatibilizers, poly(proylene)’s yield stress increased
substantially with increasing content of grafted maleic
anhydride. In comparison to poly(propylene) with a mod-
ulus of 1490 MPa and a yield stress of 33 MPa, the corre-
sponding compound (sample 10C12/20H, Tab. 2), con-
taining 20 wt.-% H type PP-g-MA compatibilizer with a
high maleic anhydride content of 4.2 wt.-% and silicate
MEC12 modified with dodecylammonium cations exhib-
ited a modulus of 3240 MPa and a yield stress of 43
MPa, whereas the corresponding compound based upon
the shorter C8 modifier (10C8/20H, Tab. 2) gave only
modulus of 2490 MPa and a yield stress of 37 MPa. Since
18.4 wt.-% of the MEC12 filler corresponded to the C12
amine, approximately 8 wt.-% of silicate afforded modu-
lus increases similar to those obtained by adding more
Young’s modulus as a function of the filler content of compounds of MEC8 and MEC12
Yield stress as a function of the filler content of compounds of MEC8 and MEC12
Poly(propylene)/organoclay nanocomposite formation: Influence of compatibilizer ...
The significant impact of the alkyl chain length of the
silicate modifier and the compatibilizer addition is appar-
ent when comparing compounds, containing 10 wt.-%
MECn filler with various alkyl chain length of the silicate
modifier, which were prepared in the presence and
absence of 20 wt.-% H type compatibilizer. In the
absence of compatibilizer both Young’s modulus (cf.
Fig. 4) and yield stress (cf. Fig. 5) were very similar.
However, when good interfacial coupling was achieved
by adding 20 wt.-% H type compatibilizer, both Young’s
modulus and yield stress increased significantly when
MEC12 was used instead of MEC8. From Tab. 2 it is
apparent that compatibilized compounds exhibited rather
poor impact resistance. This is due to the very high con-
tent of PP-g-MA compatibilizer. At high maleic anhy-
dride content, the free radical maleic anhydride grafting
process is accompanied by chain scission and low mole-
cular mass of the corresponding PP-g-MA. High content
of such oligomers can adversely affect impact perfor-
mance. Moreover, at high maleic anhydride content
incompatibility problems could arise. Lower compatibili-
zer content, higher compatibilizer molar mass, or prefer-
ably rubber addition would be required to improve the
toughness/stiffness balance of such compounds. Further,
a better interfacial adhesion between filler and matrix,
which is realized by the compatibilizer, reduces the
deformability and thus the toughness.
Since it was expected that the influence of alkyl chain
length in the presence of compatibilizer is attributable to
changes of superstructures, WAXS, AFM, and TEM stu-
dies were performed to elucidate the role of superstruc-
ture formation. Fig. 6 and 7 display the WAXS traces of
modified silicates and the corresponding poly(propylene)
compounds in the presence and absence of E and H type
compatibilizer. Clearly, the interlayer distance of MEC4
did not change upon melt-compounding and increased
only marginally when compatibilizers were present. In
fact, this WAXS pattern clearly indicates that MEC4 is
likely to behave as a conventional filler that is not exfo-
Young’s modulus as a function of the chain length of
the interlayer ammonium cation. Comparison of compounds pre-
pared in the absence and presence of H type compatibilizer
Yield stress as a function of the chain length of the
interlayer ammonium cation. Comparison of compounds pre-
pared in the absence and presence of H type compatibilizer
WAXS traces of the composites of MEC4 prepared
with and without compatibilizer addition. a) MEC4; b) 5C4/
WAXS traces of the composites and nanocomposites of
MEC12 prepared with and without compatibilizer addition. a)
MEC12; b) 5C12/20H; c) 5C12/20E; d) 5C12
liated during processing. In sharp contrast, the shapes of
the WAXS traces of MEC12-based compounds changed
dramatically as a function of compound composition. In
the absence of compatiblizer the silicate interlayer dis-
tance decreased with respect to that of the pure filler
accounts for much broader signals. Most likely, this
broadening results from exfoliation of the silicate during
processing. This behavior is also visible in Fig. 8 where
WAXS traces are displayed for 5Cn/20H compounds. For
MEC18-based compounds the original signal correspond-
ing to the silicate interlayer spacing disappears. From
Fig. 7 and 8 it is apparent that exfoliation is not complete
for MEC12 and MEC16 based compounds. From WAXS
analyses it is possible to conclude that the changes of
properties reported above for MEC12-, MEC16-, and
MEC18-based compounds with respect to the properties
of MEC4-, MEC6- and MEC8-based compounds are
clearly related to morphological changes and increased
interlayer distance resulting from exfoliation of the sili-
cate layers during processing.
The dispersion of the silicate particles was studied by
means of atomic force microscopy (AFM). The AFM
micrographs of the etched sample 5C4/20H is displayed
in Fig. 9 where a few large silicate particles, very similar
in size to the original MEC4 filler particles, are dispersed
within the poly(propylene) matrix. Much finer disper-
sions and formation of skeleton-like superstructures are
found for the corresponding compounds containing 5
wt.-% of MEC12 (cf. Fig. 10). This dispersion of much
finer particles is the result of exfoliation, which promotes
break-up of the original filler particles and self-assembly
of the resulting much smaller particles. Such in-situ
formed particles have an average diameter of approxi-
mately 1.5 lm. The thickness of the samples cannot be
determined via this method because the samples were
molten under little pressure between two cover glasses
before etching, so that the layers of the silicates were
arranged facing to the surface of the sample holder. In the
absence of compatibilizer it was impossible to achieve
such fine dispersions. For comparison, Fig. 11 displays
the AFM micrograph of the corresponding non-compati-
lized compound 5C12 which was obtained by melt-com-
pounding in the absence of compatibilizer. The silicate
particles in this composite formed very large clusters
exceeding 10 lm in size. No exfoliated particles were
detected and the average particle size of the primary par-
ticles varied between 1 and 5 lm.
Micro- and nanometer-scaled morphology develop-
ment was also monitored as a function of alkyl chain
length and compatibilizer addition using transmission
electron microscopy on thin sections. Fig. 12 displays the
compatibilized poly(propylene) compound based upon 5
wt.-% MEC4 (sample 5C4/20H, Tab. 2). Fairly large
MEC4 particles with average length of more than 4 lm
and width of about 1 lm are visisble. It is apparent from
WAXS traces of the poly(propylene) compounds pre-
pared in the presence of H type compatibilizer and various orga-
nophilic silicates: a) 5MEC4/20H; b) 5MEC6/20H; c) 5MEC8/
20H; d) 5MEC12/20H; e) 5MEC16/20H; f) 5MEC18/20H

Fig. 12 and 13 that compounds based upon MECn fillers
with n A 8 exhibit completely different architectures. The
TEM micrograph of the sample 5C12/20H shows uni-
formly dispersed nanosilicates. Such nanostructure for-

enhanced, thus producing nano-scaled particles consisting
dimensions. The length of such particles is about 500 nm
and the average thickness is just a few nanometers, corre-
sponding to the thickness of silicate layer stacks. This
high aspect ratio is one of the keys to achieve reinforce-
ment of the poly(propylene) matrix. It should be noted
that the morphologies in Fig. 13 and 14 are very similar,
because the visible differences result form different
observation angles during TEM imgaging. The TEM
study reveals that in-situ formation of silicate nanoparti-
cles with high aspect ratio, obtained during melt com-
pounding of poly(propylene) together with organophilic
silicates MECn with n A 8, accounts for improved stiff-
ness and strength. The in-situ formed silicate nanoparti-
cles are interconnected, as evidenced by their skeleton-
Modification of water-swellable layered silicates by
means of ion exchange with protonated alkyl amines in
combination with PP-g-MA compatibilizer addition dur-
ing processing represents the key to the preparation of
poly(propylene) nanocomposites and in-situ formation of
silicate nanoparticles with a high aspect ratio. The alkyl
chain length must exceed 8 C atoms in order to achieve
nanocomposite formation, which is also promoted by
increasing anhydride functionality of the PP-g-MA com-
patibilizer. Although the nature of the interfacial coupling
reactions requires further clarification, hydrogen bridges
as well as covalent, hydrophobic, and ionic bond forma-
tions at the interfaces between filler and poly(propylene)
AFM images of an etched sample of 5C12/20H. Left: height mode; right: phase
AFM images of an etched sample of 5C12. Left: height mode; right: phase mode
are required to transfer stresses during processing in order
to achieve improved exfoliation and stabilization of uni-
formly dispersed anisotropic nanoparticle. Unique combi-
nations of improved stiffness and strength are only
obtained when nanostructures are formed. On-going
research is aimed at the role of nanoparticle orientation,
nanovoid formation during deformation, and characteri-
The controlled in-situ formation of skeleton-like sili-
cate superstructures and the in-situ conversion of bulk
poly(propylene) into interfacial poly(propylene) offers
attractive opportunities for tailoring novel poly(propyl-
ene) materials without changing the polymerization pro-
